title: R-03 follow-up: cross-tier inheritance for per-project canopy roles
description:
R-03 (pl-fef5) shipped per-project .canopy/ tier with single-tier inheritance only. Canopy already supports inheritance within a tier; the resolver needs to look up the parent across tiers so a project-tier role can `extend:` a library or built-in role.

Sketch:
- Extend the canopy render flow to walk parent references across tiers (project > library > built-in lookup) instead of the current single-cwd resolve.
- Source-stamping: the rendered row's source label tracks the leaf tier (project:<id>), not the parent — already correct under R-03.
- Open question: collision semantics when a per-project role and its library parent share a name. Per-project wins for the leaf; parent resolution must walk past name shadows.
- Acceptance: spawn a project-tier role that extends a built-in via canopy frontmatter, render succeeds, runs.rendered_agent_json carries the merged sections.

References: ROADMAP R-03 open questions; SPEC §8.1 (`POST /agents/refresh`); plan pl-fef5 step 5 (refreshProjectAgents).

title: R-03 follow-up: on-disk rendered cache for project-tier roles
description:
R-03 (pl-fef5) shipped per-project .canopy/ tier but deferred the on-disk rendered cache. Today only warren can see what a project-tier role resolves to; consumers without warren (e.g. `cn render` outside the container, agents reading the project directly) can't see the rendered output.

Sketch:
- Per refreshProjectAgents, write the rendered JSON to `<projectPath>/.canopy/.rendered/<name>.json` alongside the agents-table cache update.
- Mirrors `cn emit`'s contract: the rendered artifact is git-trackable and visible to non-warren consumers.
- Open question: whether `.rendered/` should be gitignored by default (recompute on clone) or committed (audit trail / cross-tool consumption). Probably gitignored — matches canopy's existing emit ergonomics.
- Acceptance: `POST /projects/:id/agents/refresh` populates .canopy/.rendered/*.json; the warren UI's existing source-label semantics are unchanged; `cn render` outside warren reads the cache without re-resolving.

References: ROADMAP R-03 open questions; SPEC §8.1; plan pl-fef5 step 5 (refreshProjectAgents).

title: preview: split setup from command for separate timeout + future eager-boot
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 4 of plan pl-acbf.

Parent seed: warren-4554 — Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Adopt the five existing follow-up seeds into one feature plan and land them in ascending blast radius. Bug fixes ship first (warren-a743 is a one-line gate removal; warren-33eb adds an AbortController to one fetch call) so dogfooding stops…

Run `sd plan show pl-acbf` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Motivation

Closing warren-0928 raised `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` to 5m and plumbed a per-project `readiness_timeout` so cold `pnpm install` no longer trips the readiness probe. That fixes the symptom but conflates two distinct phases — dependency install and dev-server bind — under one timeout and one failure mode (`readiness_timeout`). Operators reading `preview_failure_message` can't tell whether their server failed to bind or their install crashed.

Splitting `setup` from `command` is also the schema prerequisite for the bigger win: running install *during* the agent run (Phase 2 / separate seed) so the preview goes live in seconds at reap time instead of minutes.

## Scope (Phase 1 — this seed)

Run the setup command synchronously at reap-time preview launch, before the dev-server sidecar. Phase 2 (eager / boot-time setup) is filed separately.

### 1. Schema (`src/warren-config/schema.ts`)

Add to `ServerPreviewConfigSchema`:
- `setup: z.string().min(1).optional()` — shell command, same `sh -c` execution shape as `command`.
- `setup_timeout: PreviewSetupTimeoutSchema.optional()` — reuse the duration grammar; bound 1s..1h; default 5m.

Strict object stays strict; both fields are optional so existing projects (`command: pnpm install && pnpm run dev`) keep working without migration.

### 2. Launcher (`src/preview/launch.ts`)

Insert a pre-step between port allocation and the dev-server sidecar spawn:

1. Allocate port (unchanged).
2. If `previewConfig.setup` is set:
   - `sidecars.create({ command: ["sh", "-c", setup] })` with NO `inboundPortForward`.
   - Poll sidecar state until it reports `exited`, bounded by `setup_timeout`.
   - On non-zero exit code → `attachPreview({ state: 'failed', message: 'setup failed: <stderr tail>' })`, return `{ ok: false, reason: 'setup_failed' }`.
   - On timeout → same shape, `reason: 'setup_timeout'`. Best-effort `sidecars.delete` to terminate the lingering setup process.
3. Spawn the dev-server sidecar with `inboundPortForward` (unchanged).
4. Readiness probe loop (unchanged).

New `LaunchFailureReason` variants: `"setup_failed" | "setup_timeout"`.

Once split, the readiness probe only covers dev-server bind. `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` can drop back from 5m → 60s in a follow-up — leave that bump for a separate change so this seed is purely additive.

### 3. Burrow-client surface (`src/burrow-client/`)

`PreviewSidecarsClient` currently exposes `create` / `logs` / `delete` only (`src/preview/launch.ts:57-72`). The setup step needs a way to observe sidecar exit. Burrow's server already tracks sidecar state internally — add a `status(burrowId, sidecarId)` method to the typed facade that returns `{ state: 'running' | 'exited' | ..., exitCode: number | null }`. Don't talk to the socket directly; mirror via the HttpClient facade per the warren↔burrow boundary rule.

### 4. Reap (`src/runs/reap.ts:551`)

Pass `setup` and `setup_timeout` through alongside `readinessTimeoutMs`. Same `parseDurationMs` pattern as warren-0928.

### 5. Tests

- `src/warren-config/schema.test.ts`: accepts `setup` + `setup_timeout`; rejects bad durations and empty `setup`.
- `src/preview/launch.test.ts`:
  - setup success → dev-server sidecar spawns and probe runs as today
  - setup non-zero exit → no dev-server sidecar created; `reason: 'setup_failed'`; stderr tail in `preview_failure_message`
  - setup hangs past `setup_timeout` → `reason: 'setup_timeout'`; setup sidecar deleted
  - no `setup` field → existing path, unchanged
- `src/runs/reap.test.ts`: config setup/timeout reach the launcher.

### 6. Docs

Add the new fields to the commented stub in `.warren/preview.yaml`:

\`\`\`yaml
# setup: pnpm install        # optional — runs once before `command`. Non-zero
#                            # exit fails the preview before the dev server is
#                            # attempted; stderr tail captured into
#                            # preview_failure_message.
# setup_timeout: 5m          # optional — duration, 1s..1h, default 5m. Sized
#                            # for cold pnpm/npm installs.
\`\`\`

## Out of scope (file separately)

- **Eager / boot-time setup** (`setup_when: boot`): kick off the setup sidecar right after `burrows.up` so install runs concurrently with the agent run, and reap-time preview goes live in seconds. Touches `src/runs/spawn.ts`, a new `preview_setup_sidecar_id` column, and reap. Phase 2.
- **Persistent install cache** across runs (shared pnpm/npm store volume). Still the highest-ROI cold-install fix but a separate design (cache locality, invalidation, package manager selection).
- **Dropping `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` back to 60s** once setup is split out. Trivially additive once Phase 1 ships; leave for a follow-up so this seed stays scope-disciplined.

## References

- closed parent: warren-0928 (timeout plumb-through + 5m default)
- launcher: `src/preview/launch.ts:115` (default), `:131-208` (orchestration)
- reap call site: `src/runs/reap.ts:551-568`
- duration parser: `src/preview/duration.ts`
- schema: `src/warren-config/schema.ts` (ServerPreviewConfigSchema)
- burrow boundary: warren `CLAUDE.md` "Relationship to burrow" — facade-only access to sidecars

title: preview: inject HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 (+HOST/PORT) into sidecar env so Next.js / Vite bind reachably
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 3 of plan pl-acbf.

Parent seed: warren-4554 — Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Adopt the five existing follow-up seeds into one feature plan and land them in ascending blast radius. Bug fixes ship first (warren-a743 is a one-line gate removal; warren-33eb adds an AbortController to one fetch call) so dogfooding stops…

Run `sd plan show pl-acbf` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Problem

run_ck7nz9z771b2 and run_m2ng8zd7f6m4 (jayminwest.com, Next.js 14.2.24) both timed out on the readiness probe even though the dev server logged `✓ Ready`. Burrow's inbound forwarder (`../burrow/src/provider/local/inbound-forward.ts:118-125`) enters the netns and connects via `nc 127.0.0.1 <sandboxPort>`. If the dev server binds to `localhost`/`::1` only (Next.js 13.5+ default), the forwarder can't reach it.

Per-project workaround on jayminwest.com is now `pnpm exec next dev -H 0.0.0.0` (commit 810733f / follow-up commit). That's a sticker over a class-of-bug warren should absorb.

## Why env injection alone is not enough

First instinct was to inject `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` / `HOST=0.0.0.0` via `PreviewSidecarsClient.create.env`. Verified against `node_modules/next/dist/bin/next` (Next.js 14.2.24): the commander option `-H, --hostname` is NOT chained with `.env(...)`, while `-p, --port` IS (`.env("PORT")`). So **Next.js's CLI silently ignores HOSTNAME/HOST env vars** — only the `-H` flag works.

Other dev servers behave differently:

| CLI                         | hostname source                              |
|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `next dev` (14.2)          | `-H` flag only (env IGNORED)                 |
| `vite`                     | `--host` flag, `vite.config.server.host`     |
| `react-scripts start`      | `HOST` env var                                |
| `webpack-dev-server`       | `--host` flag, config                        |
| `parcel`                   | `--host` flag                                |
| Express-style custom       | whatever the project writes                  |

Env injection helps CRA but is a no-op for the framework that triggered the bug. We need a hybrid.

## Proposal

Three layers, additive:

1. **Default env block on the sidecar** (`src/preview/launch.ts:151-158`):

   \`\`\`ts
   env: {
       HOST: "0.0.0.0",
       HOSTNAME: "0.0.0.0",
       PORT: String(input.previewConfig.port),
   }
   \`\`\`

   Helps CRA / Express-style. Cheap. Project commands can still override by setting their own inline (`HOST=10.0.0.1 pnpm dev`).

2. **Doc the framework reality** in the `.warren/preview.yaml` stub: framework matrix above so operators know the right knob. `next dev` → `-H 0.0.0.0`. `vite` → `--host 0.0.0.0`. Etc.

3. **(Optional, larger)** Readiness diagnostic: if the probe ever times out *after* the sidecar has been running for > N seconds AND `captureFailureTail` shows Next.js's "Ready in Xs" string, append a "did your dev server bind to 0.0.0.0?" hint to `preview_failure_message`. Surfaces the bug class instead of making every operator rediscover it. Worth its own follow-up — out of scope here.

## Risks

- A dev server that interprets `HOST` as "upstream API host" misbehaves. Document the override pattern; project owns their `command`.
- A project that needs IPv6-only binding gets surprised. Edge case; mitigated by the override path.

## Plan

1. `src/preview/launch.ts:151-158`: thread the env block through `sidecars.create`. The `PreviewSidecarsClient.env?: Record<string, string>` interface is already declared (`src/preview/launch.ts:61`).

2. `src/preview/launch.test.ts`: assert the three env vars are forwarded; assert `PORT` matches `previewConfig.port`.

3. Update the `.warren/preview.yaml` stub doc with the framework matrix and the override pattern.

4. Roll forward jayminwest.com once warren ships: leave the explicit `-H 0.0.0.0` (Next.js needs it regardless) and the `HOST=`/`HOSTNAME=` prefix becomes redundant for that project — fine to drop.

## References

- failed runs: `run_ck7nz9z771b2` (2026-05-15T17:39:28Z–17:44:28Z), `run_m2ng8zd7f6m4` (2026-05-15T17:51:18Z–17:56:18Z). Both showed `✓ Ready in Xs` followed by probe timeout.
- Next.js CLI evidence: `node_modules/next/dist/bin/next:83` — `-H, --hostname` declared WITHOUT `.env(...)`; `-p, --port` declared WITH `.env("PORT")`.
- burrow forwarder: `../burrow/src/provider/local/inbound-forward.ts:118-125` (the `nc 127.0.0.1 <sandboxPort>` line).
- launcher: `src/preview/launch.ts:151-158`.
- facade: `PreviewSidecarsClient.create.env` (`src/preview/launch.ts:61`).
- workaround commit: jayminwest.com `.warren/preview.yaml` → `pnpm exec next dev -H 0.0.0.0`.
- prior failure-mode seeds: warren-a82b (Bun node shim), warren-0928 (timeout), warren-d9e7 (open: split setup from command).
closeReason: Default sidecar env (HOST/HOSTNAME/PORT) injected in src/preview/launch.ts; .warren/preview.yaml stub now documents the framework matrix and the inline-override pattern. Tests assert all three vars and PORT=previewConfig.port.

title: preview: probe fetch has no per-call timeout — a single hung response blocks the readiness deadline
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 2 of plan pl-acbf.

Parent seed: warren-4554 — Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Adopt the five existing follow-up seeds into one feature plan and land them in ascending blast radius. Bug fixes ship first (warren-a743 is a one-line gate removal; warren-33eb adds an AbortController to one fetch call) so dogfooding stops…

Run `sd plan show pl-acbf` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Symptom

`run_7jjpt2jn9ej5` (jayminwest.com, deployed warren) transitioned `preview_state: starting → live` at 18:19:46 UTC — **6m 47s** after `preview_started_at: 2026-05-15T17:51:18.674Z`. The configured `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` is 300_000 (5m, warren-0928). The probe should have failed at 18:17:59 but didn't.

The fly logs show no `reap step failed` event for this run — the loop genuinely waited past 5m for a single `fetch()` to resolve, then succeeded.

## Root cause

`src/preview/launch.ts:211-225` (`probeOnce`):

\`\`\`ts
async function probeOnce(fetchImpl: typeof fetch, url: string): Promise<boolean> {
    try {
        const res = await fetchImpl(url, { method: "GET", redirect: "manual" });
        ...
\`\`\`

No `AbortController`, no `signal`, no per-call timeout. If Bun's fetch hangs waiting for response bytes (e.g. burrow forwarder accepted the TCP connection but `nc` relay hasn't started passing data because Next.js is mid-compile, or keep-alive holds the connection open after a partial response), `probeOnce` blocks indefinitely.

The deadline check at `src/preview/launch.ts:190-205` only runs **after** `probeOnce` returns. Loop structure:

\`\`\`ts
while (true) {
    const ready = await probeOnce(...);   // can block indefinitely
    if (ready) { ...live, return... }
    if (now().getTime() >= deadline) { ...failed, return... }
    await sleep(pollMs);
}
\`\`\`

So `readiness_timeout` is effectively a *wall-clock bound on the deadline check between calls*, not on probe duration. One hung probe and the bound is meaningless.

## Why this didn't show up earlier

Pre-warren-a82b runs failed inside ~60s on Bun's `node:sqlite` crash — the relay never started, every fetch got connection-refused fast, the loop iterated cleanly past the deadline. Same shape for the cold-pnpm-install timeout (warren-0928). It took a run that actually reached the dev-server-bound state to expose this: `nc` connected, Next.js was compiling a fresh route, fetch waited.

## Plan

1. **Wrap each fetch in an `AbortController`** scoped to a short per-call timeout. 2s is a reasonable upper bound — a dev server that takes >2s to send any bytes for `GET /` is not "ready" in the §11.L sense even if it's alive.

   \`\`\`ts
   async function probeOnce(fetchImpl: typeof fetch, url: string): Promise<boolean> {
       const ac = new AbortController();
       const tid = setTimeout(() => ac.abort(), PROBE_PER_CALL_TIMEOUT_MS);
       try {
           const res = await fetchImpl(url, {
               method: "GET",
               redirect: "manual",
               signal: ac.signal,
           });
           ...
       } catch {
           return false;
       } finally {
           clearTimeout(tid);
       }
   }
   \`\`\`

   `PROBE_PER_CALL_TIMEOUT_MS` lives next to the other constants; default 2_000.

2. **Update `launch.test.ts`**: add a test that injects a fetch which hangs for 10s, asserts the loop transitions to `failed` at the wall-clock deadline (not 10s later). Existing tests inject synchronous fetches so they don't exercise this.

3. **No schema change.** The per-call timeout is an internal implementation detail; operators tune the outer wall-clock via `readiness_timeout`.

## Out of scope (file separately)

- Drop `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` back from 5m to ~60s once warren-d9e7 (split setup from command) ships — once install is its own sub-step the dev-server probe wall clock no longer needs to budget for pnpm.

## References

- run id: `run_7jjpt2jn9ej5`
- launcher: `src/preview/launch.ts:121` (constant), `:180-208` (loop), `:211-225` (probeOnce)
- prior failure-mode seeds: warren-a82b, warren-0928, warren-d9e7
- diagnosis transcript: 2026-05-15 session (Claude Opus 4.7), confirmed `preview_state='live'` only after fetch resolved past wall-clock deadline.

title: preview: drop sqlite-only gate in /runs/:id/preview/teardown handler — RunPreviewsRepo is already dialect-polymorphic
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 1 of plan pl-acbf.

Parent seed: warren-4554 — Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Adopt the five existing follow-up seeds into one feature plan and land them in ascending blast radius. Bug fixes ship first (warren-a743 is a one-line gate removal; warren-33eb adds an AbortController to one fetch call) so dogfooding stops…

Run `sd plan show pl-acbf` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Symptom

Operator clicks the **Teardown** affordance in the UI against a live preview on the warren-deployed (postgres-backed) instance and gets a `503` with:

\`\`\`
preview teardown is sqlite-only today (dialect=postgres); see SPEC §11.L pl-f17e follow-up
\`\`\`

Diagnosed 2026-05-15 against `run_7jjpt2jn9ej5` on the warren-deployed instance — the run reached `preview_state='live'` but the manual teardown route refuses to execute on the postgres deploy. The eviction worker would still TTL it out eventually, but operators have no way to free the port (and the live row holding it) on demand.

## Root cause

`src/server/handlers.ts:1019-1056` (`previewTeardownHandler`) hard-gates on dialect:

\`\`\`ts
if (deps.db === undefined || deps.db.dialect !== "sqlite") {
    return jsonResponse(503, {
        error: {
            code: "preview_teardown_unavailable",
            message: ... "preview teardown is sqlite-only today (dialect=" + deps.db.dialect + "); see SPEC §11.L pl-f17e follow-up",
        },
    });
}
\`\`\`

That gate is **vestigial**. The underlying `createRunPreviewsRepo` was ported to dialect-polymorphic under closed seed `warren-adfb`. `src/preview/eviction.ts:535-556` is explicit:

> Construct a dialect-polymorphic previews repo over either sqlite or pg (warren-adfb)…  The pg branch grabs `SELECT … FOR UPDATE` on the candidate row to serialize against another claimer or the eviction worker; sqlite single-connection naturally serializes within one process…

So `previews.claimTeardown` (the CAS that teardown depends on) already works on pg — the eviction worker exercises that path in prod every tick.

What the handler is missing is just removing the dialect gate (and the matching boot-time check, if any, that constructs `deps.db` differently for non-sqlite).

## Why this lingered

`warren-adfb` widened the repo construction; it did **not** sweep callers that pre-gated themselves on `dialect === "sqlite"`. The matching gate on the eviction route at `src/server/handlers.ts:1263` should be audited at the same time — eviction *worker* runs on pg today but the manual route may have the same defensive 503.

## Plan

1. **Drop the gate.** `src/server/handlers.ts:1019-1035`: remove the `deps.db === undefined || deps.db.dialect !== "sqlite"` branch and the 503 envelope. Replace with the narrower preconditions actually needed (`deps.db === undefined` → 503 with `preview_teardown_unavailable: this warren has no db handle wired`).

2. **Audit the sibling route.** `src/server/handlers.ts:1263` (eviction-driven endpoint, whatever it is) — same treatment if it gates on dialect.

3. **Acceptance.** Extend `scripts/acceptance/scenarios/20-preview.ts` (or a sibling) to exercise manual teardown on the pg backend. Today it's skip-gated for pg (mx-994fa5 / warren-0f34); this specific path doesn't need the broader repo widening since the previews repo is the one already done.

4. **Tests.** `src/server/handlers.test.ts` (or wherever the teardown route is tested): add a postgres-shaped test case that hits the route with `deps.db.dialect === "postgres"` and asserts `200 { tornDown: true, … }` rather than 503.

5. **Operator UX.** The UI's teardown button (warren-c0b9 / warren-016d) currently silently fails against the 503; once the route returns 200, the existing `RunDetail` flow lights up. No UI change needed.

## Out of scope (file separately)

- The broader repo widening (`warren-0f34`) — that unblocks scenario 19 end-to-end. This seed is the narrower fix for the *one* repo that already supports pg.

## References

- failing handler: `src/server/handlers.ts:1025-1035`
- dialect-polymorphic previews repo: `src/preview/eviction.ts:535-556` (`createRunPreviewsRepo`)
- sibling eviction route: `src/server/handlers.ts:1263`
- closed parent: `warren-adfb` (RunPreviewsRepo port to pg)
- live test run: `run_7jjpt2jn9ej5` (warren-deployed, preview live at 30000, blocked manual teardown 2026-05-15T18:30Z)
- related open seed: `warren-0f34` (the rest of the repo layer)
closeReason: Dropped sqlite-only gate in previewTeardownHandler (src/server/handlers.ts:1025) — narrowed precondition to deps.db === undefined since createRunPreviewsRepo (warren-adfb) is already dialect-polymorphic. Audited sibling at handlers.ts:1263 (previewMaxLiveReadyzCheck): it only gates on deps.db === undefined, no change needed. Added pg-conditional regression test in handlers.preview.test.ts asserting the route works on postgres dialect; updated the existing 503-no-db-handle test title that misleadingly said 'sqlite-only'. Quality gates green (1367 pass / 16 skip / 0 fail, lint + typecheck clean).

title: preview: path-mode out-of-the-box for SPAs (referer routing + per-run cookie + abs-path HTML rewrite)
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 5 of plan pl-acbf.

Parent seed: warren-4554 — Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Adopt the five existing follow-up seeds into one feature plan and land them in ascending blast radius. Bug fixes ship first (warren-a743 is a one-line gate removal; warren-33eb adds an AbortController to one fetch call) so dogfooding stops…

Run `sd plan show pl-acbf` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Motivation

warren positions path mode (SPEC §11.L addendum, warren-f4d7) as the zero-DNS default — operators with nothing but a single host get working previews by setting `WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST=<their-host>` and shipping `.warren/preview.yaml`. **Today it half-works.** Diagnosed against `run_pexj1wxq90v0` (Next.js 14.2 on jayminwest.com, warren-deployed): the main HTML loads at `/p/<id>/`, but every root-relative asset (`/_next/static/...`, `/favicon.ico`) hits the warren root instead of the preview, fetches warren's own SPA `index.html` (847B `text/html`), and the browser tries to parse it as JS/CSS/woff2. Result: a "live" preview that renders blank.

Evidence: `warren-deployed.fly.dev.har` — 11 entries, 1 correct HTML response (155KB), 10 asset requests all returning warren's 847B SPA index.

The HTML rewriter (warren-ab3a) does inject `<base href="/p/<id>/">`, but `<base>` is spec'd to only affect *relative* URLs. Next/Vite/CRA/Remix/Astro all emit `/_next/...` style root-relative paths in their compiled output; `<base>` is irrelevant to those. SPEC §11.L addendum acknowledges this ("apps that compute URLs from `window.location.host`… misbehave under `/p/<id>/`. Operators with such apps use subdomain mode.") — but that footnote is misleading because it's nearly every modern SPA, not an edge case.

For warren-as-self-hostable-software this needs to be the default that works without operator infra. Subdomain mode stays as the explicit opt-in for BYOD operators (separate seed; that one requires real DNS work the operator owns).

## Plan — three coupled changes, all warren-side

Order matters: (1) is the biggest unlock for the smallest blast radius; (2) is the prerequisite that (1) needs to be functional under auth; (3) is defense-in-depth.

### 1. Referer-based asset routing (~50 lines in `src/preview/proxy.ts`)

When warren's main router would otherwise fall through to the SPA / 404 / static handler AND the request's `Referer` header points to `/p/<id>/…`, extract the runId from Referer and route the request to that preview's upstream port — same as if the URL had been `/p/<id>/<originalPath>`.

- Reliable signal: same-origin SPA asset loads always send Referer (browser default).
- Order: real warren routes (API, login, SPA fallback) win on path match. Only when the path would 404/fallback do we consult Referer.
- Edge case — `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`: rare on dev servers; covered by piece (3).
- Edge case — request without Referer that legitimately wants the warren root: passes through, no preview routing.

This alone fixes 90% of the broken-asset class without requiring framework cooperation.

### 2. Per-run cookie name with `Path=/` (touches `src/preview/auth.ts` + proxy auth check)

Today path-mode cookies are `warren_preview` with `Path=/p/<id>`. The browser won't ship that cookie on a request to `/_next/static/foo` (path doesn't match), so piece (1) above can't even authenticate the routed asset request.

Fix: cookie name becomes `warren_preview_<runId>` (literal name encodes the runId), `Path=/`. Per-run name guarantees cross-preview isolation; `Path=/` ensures the browser sends it on every same-origin request. Server picks the cookie matching the runId extracted from URL (`/p/<id>/...`) or Referer (asset case).

Token contents stay the same — runId is already in the JWT-style payload — the change is purely cookie naming/scope. Login route (`GET /runs/:id/preview/login`) writes the per-run cookie name.

### 3. Absolute-path rewriter as defense-in-depth (extend `applyPathModeRewrites`)

The HTML rewriter currently only injects `<base>`. Add a pass that prefixes root-relative URLs in HTML:

- `href="/..."` → `href="/p/<id>/..."`
- `src="/..."` → `src="/p/<id>/..."`
- `srcset="/foo, /bar"` → same prefixing per entry
- `<link rel="preload" as="font" href="/...">` etc.

Skip `//` (protocol-relative — already pointing elsewhere), `data:`, `http:`, `https:`, `#`, `?`. Limit to the same `HTML_HEAD_LOOKAHEAD_BYTES` cap the `<base>` injector uses (64 KiB).

Covers the case where the framework strips Referer or generates absolute paths from server-rendered HTML that piece (1) wouldn't see (e.g., a fetch from server-rendered JSON).

CSS `url(/foo)` rewriting is a follow-up — it requires content-type sniffing and parsing CSS. File separately if a real case surfaces.

## Acceptance

- `scripts/acceptance/scenarios/20-preview-path.ts` extended: spin up a Next.js (or minimal SPA stand-in that emits `/_next/static/...` style URLs) sidecar in path mode, assert all assets resolve with 200 + correct content-type, the page DOM has the expected text.
- `src/preview/proxy.test.ts`: new tests for (a) referer-based asset routing, (b) per-run cookie name parsing, (c) absolute-path HTML rewrite.
- `src/server/handlers.test.ts`: cookie-set flow uses the new per-run name.

## References

- failing run: `run_pexj1wxq90v0` (warren-deployed, jayminwest.com PR #19, live at port 30001)
- HAR evidence: `warren-deployed.fly.dev.har` (11 entries, 10 are warren's 847B SPA index served as `text/html` for `/_next/*` + `/favicon.ico`)
- proxy: `src/preview/proxy.ts:382-456` (`applyPathModeRewrites`), `:155-175` (host parser), `:483-512` (`injectBaseHref`)
- HTML rewriter parent seed: warren-ab3a (closed; only does `<base>` injection)
- SPEC §11.L addendum: `SPEC.md:1369-1505` (path mode design lock + accepted trade-offs)
- subdomain-mode polish (sibling): TBD seed for production tier
closeReason: Shipped path-mode SPA out-of-the-box support: (1) cookie name is now warren_preview_<runId> at Path=/ so the browser ships it on every same-origin asset request; (2) proxy preamble consults Referer to route asset sub-resources to the right preview when url.pathname doesn't match /p/<id>/...; (3) abs-path HTML rewriter prefixes root-relative href/src/srcset values within the 64KB head window. All three changes are coupled and tested in src/preview/proxy.test.ts; acceptance scenario 20-preview-path now asserts referer-routed asset fetching end-to-end.

title: preview: polish subdomain mode end-to-end (real acceptance scenario, doctor probes, Caddy runbook depth)
description:
## Motivation

Subdomain mode (the original SPEC §11.L design, `https://run-<id>.<host>/`) is warren's production-grade preview tier — every preview gets its own origin, root-relative URLs Just Work, cookie scope is `Domain=.<host>; Path=/`, headroom is bounded only by the port range. The code path is implemented and shipped (`src/preview/proxy.ts:121-127` config shape, `:155-175` `parseRunIdFromHost`, `formatPreviewUrl(...)` at `:342-347`).

But the **operator experience for getting there** is rough. A self-hoster who reads the README and wants subdomain mode hits a wall of unfinished edges: the acceptance suite doesn't exercise subdomain mode end-to-end against a real subdomain (scenario 20 uses `Host:` header rewriting against a single-origin test fixture, not a true wildcard host), the doctor surface doesn't probe the wildcard DNS / cert health, and the Caddy runbook in README is six lines with no troubleshooting depth.

This is the BYOD (bring-your-own-domain) production tier. Sibling seed covers the zero-DNS path-mode case.

## Scope

Tighten the path from "operator wants subdomain mode" → "subdomain mode running and verified" so the only thing the operator has to think about is the domain itself.

### 1. End-to-end acceptance against a real subdomain shape

Today `scenario 20-preview.ts` synthesizes the subdomain Host header on a single-origin warren in test mode (`Host: run-<id>.<preview-host>`). That validates the proxy preamble but not:

- Cookie domain attribute (`Domain=.<preview-host>`) actually scoping to all subdomains under the preview host (and *only* those).
- Browser-shaped redirect flow from `/runs/:id/preview/login?...` ending up at the right subdomain.
- Cross-preview cookie isolation — two concurrent runs each get their own subdomain cookie, no leakage.

Add `20-preview-subdomain.ts` (or extend the existing scenario with a true-subdomain mode) that boots warren with a stub-DNS / hosts-file shim resolving `*.preview.test → 127.0.0.1` and exercises the full origin-per-preview flow.

### 2. Doctor probes for the BYOD config

Currently `checkPreviewAuthStrength` (`src/diagnostics/checks.ts:559`) only warns when `WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST` is set with a placeholder `WARREN_API_TOKEN`. SPEC §11.L addendum line 1474 explicitly calls out wildcard DNS / DNS-01 advisories as subdomain-only — but they're not yet implemented. Add:

- `checkPreviewWildcardDns`: when `WARREN_PREVIEW_MODE=subdomain` (explicit or default), resolve `run-doctorprobe.<WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST>` and verify it points to the same address as `<WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST>`. Surface as a warning on mismatch; explicit pass-through on `NXDOMAIN` (operator hasn't wired DNS yet).
- `checkPreviewWildcardCert`: TLS handshake to `run-doctorprobe.<WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST>`, verify SAN includes a matching wildcard. Surface mismatches and self-signed.
- Both probes are gated on `WARREN_PREVIEW_MODE=subdomain`; they're no-ops in path mode (sibling seed's domain).

### 3. Caddy / Cloudflare / Nginx runbook depth

The README currently has a Cloudflare-only Caddy snippet (`README.md:180-191`). Expand to a self-contained `docs/preview-subdomain.md` (or a dedicated README section) covering:

- The wildcard CNAME / A record pattern (DNS provider walkthroughs for the top 5).
- Cert issuance via Caddy DNS-01 (snippet per supported DNS plugin).
- Alternative: Cloudflare proxied wildcard SSL (zero-config TLS, Cloudflare handles cert).
- Alternative: Nginx + certbot --dns-01.
- Smoke-test commands: `dig +short run-test.<host>` should return the warren box; `curl -v https://run-test.<host>/healthz` should hit warren's 503 (preview not found) not a TLS error.
- Common failure modes (DNS-01 plugin auth missing, CAA records blocking issuance, fly.io vs self-managed cert lifecycle).

### 4. (Stretch) Bundled reverse-proxy / TLS termination option

Some operators don't want a separate Caddy process. Investigate baking a Caddy-as-library option into warren so the operator sets `WARREN_PREVIEW_TLS_DNS_PROVIDER=cloudflare` + `WARREN_PREVIEW_TLS_API_TOKEN=...` and warren handles TLS termination directly. Filed as stretch — likely a separate follow-up seed since it adds a runtime dependency.

## Acceptance

- New scenario `20-preview-subdomain.ts` passing on Linux against a wildcard-resolving fixture.
- `warren doctor` surfaces wildcard DNS + cert advisories on a subdomain-mode deploy with broken DNS (manual verification on a staging warren box).
- `docs/preview-subdomain.md` (or README section) covers Cloudflare-proxied + Caddy-DNS01 + Nginx-certbot setups end-to-end with smoke-test commands.
- Acceptance smoke-test commands documented produce expected outputs against the SPEC.

## Out of scope (file separately)

- The bundled TLS-termination Caddy-library option (§4 above) — punt to follow-up unless investigation surfaces a tractable shape.
- DNS provider integration / automation (warren writing DNS records) — out of scope; operators bring their own provider.
- Path-mode SPA-asset fixes — sibling seed.

## References

- proxy mode discriminator: `src/preview/proxy.ts:121-137` (`PreviewProxyConfigSubdomain` / `PreviewProxyConfigPath`)
- host parser: `src/preview/proxy.ts:155-175` (`parseRunIdFromHost`)
- URL formatter: `src/preview/launch.ts:342-347` (`formatPreviewUrl`)
- existing doctor check: `src/diagnostics/checks.ts:539-580` (`checkPreviewAuthStrength`)
- SPEC §11.L addendum (mode trade-offs): `SPEC.md:1369-1505`
- README runbook (today): `README.md:164-197`
- existing acceptance: `scripts/acceptance/scenarios/20-preview.ts` (single-origin Host-rewrite fixture)
- sibling seed: path-mode-SPA-out-of-box (TBD)

title: Preview reliability & SPA support hardening (post-jayminwest.com dogfood)
description:
Tracking parent for five follow-ups discovered while dogfooding the R-19 preview against jayminwest.com (Next.js 14.2.24) on warren-deployed. The R-19 plan (pl-2c59) shipped the path-mode preview infrastructure; these seeds harden it into something a fresh-install operator can rely on for real-world SPA projects.

Children (in landing order — ascending blast radius):

1. warren-a743  bug   teardown sqlite-gate is vestigial (RunPreviewsRepo is dialect-polymorphic post-warren-adfb)
2. warren-33eb  bug   probe fetch has no per-call timeout — single hung response blocks the readiness deadline
3. warren-79b2  task  inject HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 + emit Next.js -H flag so the dev server binds reachably
4. warren-d9e7  task  split `setup` from `command` for separate timeout + future eager-boot
5. warren-63e1  task  path-mode out-of-the-box for SPAs (referer routing + per-run cookie + abs-path HTML rewrite)

Bugs first, then the structural sidecar/launcher refactors, then the SPA path-mode unlock that depends on the cleaner foundation. Each child has its own design notes; this seed exists to thread them under one plan revision so reviewers see the dogfood-driven arc as a unit.

title: Readiness-timeout: raise default to 10m, or start deadline at first probe-connect (warren-d9e7 follow-up)
description:
## Problem

run_6k1vxy1s9qgj (jayminwest.com on warren v0.3.15) hit the readiness cliff even though every component was healthy:

- pnpm install: 82.1s
- next dev bind: 2.3s ("Ready in 2.3s")
- Next.js first-compile of `/`: 200.4s (1875 modules)
- Total: ~285s vs. 300s `readiness_timeout` default — ~15s headroom

Sidecar log shows `Compiled / in 200.4s` did complete, but the readiness probe still failed at the 5m deadline. That's the project using the combined-command pattern (`pnpm install && next dev`). Migrating jayminwest.com to the warren-d9e7 setup-split (commit 39f4b96 in jayminwest.com) buys back the install budget, but the underlying warren-side cliff remains for any project where first-compile alone approaches or exceeds 5m.

Two distinct issues that compound:

1. **5m default is too tight for modern SPAs.** Next.js / Vite / SvelteKit first-compile of a moderately large app routinely runs 2-4 minutes cold. 5m is the right ceiling for "is the dev server bind responsive" but not for "did the bundler finish a cold compile of the entry route." Raising default to 10m gives an honest first-compile budget.

2. **Deadline starts at sidecar-create, not first probe-connect.** `src/preview/launch.ts:276` computes `deadline = now() + timeoutMs` right after `sidecars.create` returns. That means burrow's startup time, sidecar process spawn, shell pre-exec, dev-server import-and-bind all eat into the readiness budget before any HTTP probe has even fired. The probe's job is "wait for first 2xx," but the timer covers a strict superset. Starting the deadline at first successful TCP connect (or first probe call, whichever is simpler) would isolate readiness from sidecar-startup overhead.

## Approaches (file as alternatives, pick one)

### A. Raise default — one-line fix
- Bump `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` in `src/preview/launch.ts` from 5m to 10m.
- Update `.warren/preview.yaml` template comment to reflect new default + framework matrix guidance.
- Update SPEC §11.L and warren-config schema doc-comment.
- Cheap, ships fast. Doesn't fix the semantics issue (1), just gives more rope. Per-project override (`readiness_timeout` in preview.yaml) already works, so projects with even longer cold-starts can still extend.

### B. Change deadline semantics — start at first probe-connect
- In `src/preview/launch.ts:278-306`, defer the `deadline` computation until the first probe gets a TCP connect (any HTTP response, even non-2xx).
- Two sub-deadlines: a `connect_timeout` (could keep 5m default) covers "did anything bind on the port"; the `readiness_timeout` covers "did it return 2xx" and starts on first connect.
- More precise contract: "readiness_timeout is the bundler's budget, connect_timeout is the binder's budget."
- More invasive: tests, docs, schema. Probably worth doing IF we're already touching the surface — but a follow-up rather than an inline rewrite.

### C. Both: raise default AND change semantics
- Default new `connect_timeout: 5m`, default `readiness_timeout: 10m`, semantics per (B).
- Best long-term shape, biggest surface to touch.

Recommend A as a first patch (fast, fixes the immediate cliff for typical projects), file (B/C) as the follow-up.

## Acceptance

- A: `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS = 600_000`; existing readiness_timeout per-project override unchanged; preview.yaml template comment + SPEC §11.L call out the new default; one regression test pinning the constant.
- (B/C if pursued): scenario covers a slow-binding dev server (long delay before first connect) vs. a slow-compiling one (fast bind, long pre-2xx response) — each gets distinct failure reasons.

## References

- `src/preview/launch.ts:145` (`DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS = 300_000`)
- `src/preview/launch.ts:276-306` (deadline computation + probe loop)
- `src/warren-config/schema.ts:191` (preview schema, `readiness_timeout` field)
- jayminwest.com commit 39f4b96 (operator-side workaround)
- SPEC §11.L (preview lifecycle)
- Diagnosed in run_6k1vxy1s9qgj (jayminwest.com on warren v0.3.15)

title: Re-run / clone: one-click duplicate of an existing run with same agent/model/project/prompt
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Adopted into plan pl-df2f.

Parent seed: warren-4b01 — Batch: clear medium/low open backlog
Plan template: feature

Run `sd plan show pl-df2f` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Problem

Iterating on a preview / prompt / agent config today requires walking the full new-run dispatcher every time: project picker → agent picker → model picker → branch base → prompt textarea. When the operator is debugging a preview readiness cliff (or any flaky run) and needs to fire the same configuration four or five times, that's ~20 clicks of pure repetition. Observed during the v0.3.15 preview hardening loop: dogfooding required manually recreating runs against jayminwest.com over and over.

## Sibling: warren-4b11

warren-4b11 is the **continuation** case: "follow-up prompt against the prior run's pushed branch." This seed is the **replication** case: "fire a fresh run against `main` with the exact same dispatch config." Both share infra (server endpoint, UI affordance on terminal runs) but the workspace semantics differ:

- **Replicate (this seed):** new run, base = `main` (or whatever the prior run's configured base was), prompt + agent + model + project copied verbatim. Independent of prior run's branch — the prior run might have failed before pushing, or its branch is stale.
- **Continue (warren-4b11):** new run, base = prior run's pushed branch, prompt may be edited. Conversation/workspace continuity is the point.

UI should expose both on the terminal-run page as adjacent affordances: "Re-run from scratch" and "Continue with follow-up." Server-side they're nearly the same endpoint — the discriminator is the base-ref choice.

## Approach

`POST /runs` already exists. Extend with optional `cloneFromRunId` (mirroring warren-4b11's `continueFromRunId`). When set:
- Look up prior run's `agent`, `model`, `projectId`, `prompt`, and base-ref config.
- Spawn new run with same values, base = same base the prior run started from (not prior run's pushed branch — that's warren-4b11's semantics).
- Record parent link in `parent_run_id` (same column warren-4b11 needs — share the column).
- Distinguish the chain type via an extension or a `clone_kind` enum on the row: `replicate` vs. `continue`.

UI:
- On `/runs/:id` for terminal runs, add a "Re-run" button group: ["Re-run from scratch", "Continue with follow-up"]. Both prefill `/runs/new` with the cloned config; "from scratch" sets base to prior run's base, "follow-up" sets base to prior run's branch + opens the prompt textarea for edits.
- Optional later: a one-click "re-run again" on `/runs/:id` for a previously-cloned run that just dispatches without going through `/runs/new` (saves another click).

## Acceptance

- Operator on a terminal run can click "Re-run" and get an identical new run dispatched in one click (or one click + confirm).
- Server records the parent_run_id and the clone_kind.
- Works for every built-in agent (pi, claude-code, sapling).
- Tests in `src/runs/spawn.test.ts` cover the clone path (base-ref = prior run's base, not its pushed branch).
- Distinguishes cleanly from warren-4b11 in the API + UI.

## Out of scope

- Replicating with edits (e.g., swap the model but keep everything else). Future seed if useful.
- Bulk re-dispatch of N runs from a list page. Future seed.

## References

- `src/runs/spawn.ts` (workspace prep)
- `src/server/routes/runs.ts` (POST /runs)
- `src/db/schema.ts` (runs table — `parent_run_id` column shared with warren-4b11)
- warren-4b11 (sibling: continuation against prior branch)

title: Readiness-timeout: deadline starts at sidecar-create, should start at first probe-connect (warren-fdf2 approach B)
description:
## Problem

`warren-fdf2` shipped approach A (raise default 5m → 10m). The semantic issue underneath is still unresolved:

`src/preview/launch.ts:276` computes the deadline immediately after `sidecars.create` returns:

```ts
const deadline = now().getTime() + timeoutMs;
```

That means the `readiness_timeout` budget covers:
1. Burrow's sidecar process spawn
2. Shell `sh -c` pre-exec
3. Dev-server CLI startup (Node/Bun import graph, dependency loading)
4. Dev-server bind on the configured port
5. First HTTP probe TCP connect
6. First-route compile / SSR work
7. First 2xx response

Only (6) and (7) are the bundler's actual readiness — everything before (5) is sidecar startup overhead that varies with burrow health, image cold cache, and shell environment. The name `readiness_timeout` implies (7), but the timer is a strict superset.

Observed cost: on `run_428nktsej0yh` the first compile finished at ~10 min wall clock with first probe arriving within seconds of bind, so the difference was small. But sidecar startup variance under load (many concurrent runs, cold burrow, slow image pull) can easily eat 30s-2min, pushing borderline projects over even a 10m budget when the actual bundler took 8m.

## Approach B (full)

Introduce two distinct timeouts:

```ts
// "Did anything bind on the port?" budget. Covers shell pre-exec,
// CLI startup, port bind. Sized for sidecar startup variance.
export const DEFAULT_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_MS = 300_000; // 5m

// "Did the bound server return 2xx?" budget. Sized for bundler
// first-compile. Starts at first successful TCP connect (any HTTP
// status, even non-2xx).
export const DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS = 600_000; // 10m (unchanged from A)
```

Probe loop becomes a two-phase state machine:

- **Phase 1 (`connect`):** probe with `connect_timeout` deadline. Any HTTP response (including 4xx/5xx and timeouts that aren't ECONNREFUSED) flips to phase 2. ECONNREFUSED / EHOSTUNREACH retries within `connect_timeout`. Exhaustion surfaces a new failure reason `connect_timeout` distinct from `readiness_timeout`.
- **Phase 2 (`readiness`):** standard 2xx-or-3xx probe with `readiness_timeout` deadline starting at phase transition. Existing `readiness_timeout` failure-reason kept.

## Schema + config surface

`PreviewServerConfig`:
- Add `connect_timeout?: string` (duration, 1s..1h bounds — same shape as `readiness_timeout`).
- `readiness_timeout` keeps existing semantics from operator POV (a number of seconds to wait for 2xx), but server-side it's bound to the post-connect phase.

`.warren/preview.yaml` template gets a paragraph and a new field example.

## Failure surface

New `LaunchPreviewFailureReason`: `connect_timeout`. Distinct from `readiness_timeout` (which keeps meaning "server bound but couldn't serve a 2xx in time"). The two reasons are operator-actionable in different ways:

- `connect_timeout` → check the sidecar's command (binding wrong host? port mismatch?), check burrow forwarder.
- `readiness_timeout` → check the dev server's first-compile cost, possibly raise `readiness_timeout`.

`preview_failure_message` includes the failed phase so operators can tell which timeout was the actual cap.

## Tests

- New scenario covers a slow-binding fixture (e.g., `sh -c "sleep 30 && python3 -m http.server 3000"`) — confirms `connect_timeout` fires distinctly from `readiness_timeout`.
- Existing readiness_timeout tests stay correct (fixtures bind quickly, so phase transition happens immediately).
- Bun fetch behavior on `ECONNREFUSED` vs. `EHOSTUNREACH` vs. AbortController-on-no-connect needs verification — the phase-1 discriminator depends on distinguishing "bound and slow" from "not yet bound." Today's `probeOnce` returns `false` for both; we need to surface the underlying network error.

## Acceptance

- `PreviewServerConfig.connect_timeout` parses, defaults to 5m, 1s..1h bounds.
- `LaunchPreviewFailureReason` includes `connect_timeout`.
- Probe loop is two-phase; `readiness_timeout` deadline starts at first connect, not sidecar create.
- `preview_failure_message` records which phase failed.
- New acceptance scenario exercises both failure modes against deterministic fixtures.
- Migration note in CHANGELOG: existing per-project `readiness_timeout` values stay valid but now mean less than before (they only cover bundler, not bind); operators can shrink them if their dev server binds in <5m.

## References

- Sibling approach A: shipped 2026-05-17 (`warren-fdf2` original, commit 0e334a5).
- `src/preview/launch.ts:143` — `DEFAULT_READINESS_TIMEOUT_MS` (current 10m).
- `src/preview/launch.ts:274-306` — probe loop where deadline is computed.
- `src/warren-config/schema.ts:194-195` — `readiness_timeout` schema (where `connect_timeout` would join).
- `src/preview/launch.ts:127-134` — `LaunchPreviewFailureReason` enum.
- SPEC §11.L (preview lifecycle) — needs a paragraph on the two-phase contract.

## Why not now

Approach A's 10m default is sufficient for every project we've actually dogfooded. B is the right *contract* but no concrete project has been blocked on it; ship when either (a) a project trips the cliff approach A didn't cover, or (b) someone is already touching `src/preview/launch.ts` for a related reason and can fold in the two-phase rewrite at near-zero marginal cost.

title: acceptance scenario 20 + 24 hit bridge terminal-detect race when inner stub agent exits in <100ms
description:
Local repro: scenario 20-preview.ts (and the new 24-preview-node-runtime.ts) consistently fail with 'run did not reach terminal within 30000ms (last state=running)' in this sandbox.

Symptoms from WARREN_ACCEPTANCE_WARREN_STDOUT logs:
  t+0.543  burrow:  run started
  t+0.574  burrow:  run finalized (succeeded, exitCode 0) — 31ms wall
  t+0.768  warren:  bridge transitioned queued → running

Bridge subscribed ~200ms after burrow already finalized, so terminalDetected never fires and reap is never called inline (src/server/bridges.ts:254). Run state stays at 'running' forever.

This is independent of warren-0c8e — both scenario 20 (python http.server, baseline) and scenario 24 (node http) hit the same failure. Other in-proc scenarios (04, 06, 09) pass because they don't boot a second inner warren+burrow stack.

Hypothesis: when bootInProc spawns a fresh warren in the inner stack with WARREN_STUB_SLEEP_MS=0, the run-spawn → bridge-subscribe window is wider than the agent's exit, so the bridge attaches after burrow's events stream has already closed without a replay.

Suggested investigation: confirm burrow's events stream replays from seq 0 to late subscribers (or that warren's bridge can fetch terminal state via /runs/:id GET after a missed terminalDetected) — if neither is true, the bridge needs an after-attach reconciler.

Acceptance:
- scenario 20-preview.ts passes locally (current ground truth in CI)
- scenario 20-preview-path.ts passes locally
- scenario 24-preview-node-runtime.ts passes locally

title: Adopt Plot as warren's coordination primitive; shift UI from run-centric to Plot-centric
description:
## Context

Plot is an os-eco primitive shipped from `../plot/` (npm: `@os-eco/plot-cli`, CLI: `plot`). It is a typed, queryable JSON coordination object that holds the intent, attachments, and event log for a unit of work — and references (does not embed) the seeds issues, agent runs, PRs, and mulch records that constitute its substance. **Plot v1 has shipped.** See `../plot/SPEC.md` for the design and `../plot/README.md` for the CLI/library surface.

Plot exists because warren's current primary UI unit is the **run** (orchestrator → worker). The post-V1 direction outlined in [Agentic Networks](https://www.jayminwest.com/blog/12-agentic-networks) requires a different primary unit: a **shared substrate** where humans and agents coexist as peer nodes. Plot is that substrate.

## What this epic tracks

Warren's adoption of Plot as a dependency and the resulting UI / dispatch / event-flow changes. **Building Plot itself happens in the `plot/` repo and is not in scope here.**

## Scope (warren-side)

- **Dependency.** Add `@os-eco/plot-cli` (note: `-cli` suffix; both CLI and library export from the same package). Pin to the published v1 minor.
- **Dispatch path.** When a run is dispatched, set `PLOT_ID` and `PLOT_ACTOR` (`agent:<name>:<run-id>`) in the sandbox env. Agents prime context via `plot get --view implementer` — never load the full Plot.
- **Event flow.** Agent-emitted `decision_made` / `question_posed` / `artifact_produced` events stream into the Plot's event log via `plot append`, mirrored into warren's existing event stream so the run timeline and Plot timeline stay in lockstep.
- **Run reporting.** `run_dispatched` event written to the originating Plot on dispatch; run lifecycle events tagged with `plot_id` in warren's own store.
- **Write-ACL respect.** Warren never emits `intent_edited`, `status_changed`, `attachment_removed`, or `question_answered` from an `agent:*` actor — the Plot library refuses these, and we should never construct calls that would trip it. Humans transitioning a Plot's status from the warren UI is fine (warren acts on behalf of `user:<handle>`).
- **PlanRun composition (warren-fcc9).** A PlanRun launched from a Plot threads `PLOT_ID` through every child run; child events report back to the Plot; Plot auto-transitions to `done` when children close. See warren-fcc9 for the PlanRun primitive itself; this epic adds the Plot wiring (the optional `plot_id` field on PlanRun + dispatcher event hooks).
- **UI shift.** Home page becomes a list of Plots (not runs); per-Plot view has intent panel, substrate panel (attachments), unified activity feed where human and agent events look the same; "run plan" button on Plot detail dispatches a PlanRun against the attached `sd plan`.
- **Status-tag triggers (post-Plot-V1, defer).** Plot transitioning to `ready` triggers a planner agent that calls `sd plan` and writes seeds back as attachments. Out of scope for this epic — file follow-up when status-transition reliability is proven.

## Constraints

- Plot updates land on whatever branch the agent works on; propagate to main with the code (`../plot/SPEC.md` §4.3).
- Agents may never mutate intent — write-ACL enforced at the Plot library level (`../plot/SPEC.md` §6).
- Plot's JSON+JSONL files live in the repo alongside `.seeds/` and `.mulch/`; no cloud dependency.
- The SQLite index (`.plot/.index.db`) is gitignored, rebuildable from files; warren must not assume it exists on first run — call `plot rebuild-index` if needed.
- Plot integration is **gated on the project having a `.plot/` directory**, matching the existing seeds/mulch opt-in shape. Warren's core run primitive stays Plot-free.

## Sequencing

1. ✅ Plot v1 shipped from `../plot/` as `@os-eco/plot-cli` on npm.
2. **Phase 1 — Dispatch wiring (this epic, first child plan).** Add dep, thread `PLOT_ID`/`PLOT_ACTOR` env, surface `plot get --view implementer` to agents, mirror Plot events into warren's stream, write `run_dispatched` back. No UI change yet.
3. **Phase 2 — PlanRun composition.** warren-fcc9 lands as a standalone serial-plan-run feature, then grows an optional `plot_id` field so a PlanRun started from a Plot threads context through every child.
4. **Phase 3 — UI shift (largest).** Incremental: add Plot list page, add Plot detail view with "run plan" button, then deprecate run-list as the primary surface.

Phase 1 is the right place to start an `sd plan` decomposition. Phases 2 and 3 are tracked separately (Phase 2 wires into warren-fcc9; Phase 3 will be its own plan when Phase 1+2 are stable).

## Acceptance (Phase 1 only — this is what the plan should cover)

- Warren depends on `@os-eco/plot-cli` at a pinned v1 minor.
- Dispatching a run against a project with a `.plot/` directory and a Plot ID:
  - Sets `PLOT_ID` and `PLOT_ACTOR=agent:<name>:<run-id>` in the sandbox env.
  - Writes a `run_dispatched` event to the originating Plot.
  - Forwards agent-emitted Plot events (`decision_made`, `question_posed`, `artifact_produced`) into warren's event stream with `plot_id` tagging.
- Dispatching against a project **without** `.plot/` is unchanged from today.
- An agent inside the sandbox can call `plot get --view implementer` and `plot append` against the bound Plot end-to-end (verify via acceptance scenario).
- No agent-actor calls to humans-only event types ever reach the Plot library (would be a bug; the library rejects them but warren shouldn't construct them).

## Related

- **warren-fcc9** — PlanRun (serial plan execution). Independent of Plot at V1; composes with Plot when both ship. Plot wiring on PlanRun is owned by this epic, Phase 2.

## References

- `../plot/SPEC.md` — Plot v1 specification (data model §3, write-ACL §6, views §8, CLI §9, library §10)
- `../plot/README.md` — CLI/library surface, env vars (`PLOT_DIR`, `PLOT_ACTOR`, `PLOT_ID`, `PLOT_DEBUG`)
- [Agentic Networks](https://www.jayminwest.com/blog/12-agentic-networks) — theoretical foundation
- Mulch: `planning` and `ui` domains have decision records on this direction (mx-39fd5c, mx-90ad02, mx-0bd7e3 in os-eco root)

title: Detect & reconcile ghost runs (warren state running, burrow has no record)
description:
## Problem

When the warren machine restarts (e.g. `fly scale memory`, OOM kill, deploy with `immediate` strategy, host migration) and burrow loses an in-flight run from its store, the run becomes a "ghost" in the UI:

- warren's Postgres row stays in `state=running`, `ended_at=null`
- burrow returns `{"code":"not_found","message":"run not found: run_xxx"}` for the `burrow_run_id`
- UI keeps showing the run as live; user clicks **Steer** → "burrow xxx not found"; clicks **Cancel** → "run not found: run_xxx"
- warren's per-run stream bridge enters an indefinite backoff loop (`bridge errored — reconnecting after backoff`, capped at 30s/attempt), spamming logs forever

The only fix today is `UPDATE runs SET state='failed', ended_at=now() WHERE id=...` directly against Postgres, plus a process restart to stop the bridge loop. Hit live on 2026-05-17 during the 2GB→4GB scale; `run_b9dz5jdsrvsv` / burrow `run_ktz51n6n7ww3`.

## Desired behavior

A run whose `burrow_run_id` no longer exists in burrow should be reconcilable both automatically and by the user, without DB surgery.

### Reconciler (server-side)
- On warren startup, for every non-terminal run, GET burrow's run; if burrow returns 404, mark the run `failed` with `failure_reason='burrow_run_lost'` (or similar) and `ended_at=now()`.
- The per-run stream bridge should give up (not infinitely backoff) when burrow returns 404 on reconnect — the run is unrecoverable, mark it failed instead.

### UI
- When the active-run actions (Steer/Cancel) get a 404 from burrow, surface a **"Mark run failed"** affordance instead of the raw error, so the user can reconcile from the UI.
- Optionally: badge the run "stale/orphaned" once the bridge has 404'd N times.

## Acceptance
- Restart the machine mid-run (kill burrow's store deliberately for a test); the run transitions to `failed` within one reconciler tick, the bridge loop ends, and the UI lets the user dismiss it without raw burrow errors.

## Related
- Today's incident: see fly logs around `2026-05-17T19:42Z` (machine ran out of memory at 2GB → scale memory → burrow's run state lost on reboot → ghost run for `run_b9dz5jdsrvsv`).

title: Compute cost/tokens for failed runs from streamed events
description:
## Problem

When a run terminates abnormally (machine restart, ghost run, reap never finalizes), `runs.cost_usd`, `runs.tokens_input/output`, `runs.tokens_cache_read/write` are all null and the UI shows cost as `"-"`. The agent's actual token spend is invisible to the operator even though the data exists.

Concretely, today's incident: `run_b9dz5jdsrvsv` had **609 events streamed and persisted** to Postgres (454 carrying usage/cost telemetry — kinds: `state_change, telemetry, text, thinking, tool_result, tool_use`) before warren stalled and the machine rebooted. The run got far enough to almost finish, but every usage column on the row is null because reap never ran to persist totals.

Pattern in code today (warren server logs):
- During streaming, warren logs `"persisted in-stream usage totals"` periodically (seen on successful runs `run_6k1vxy1s9qgj`: $0.144 / 1726 out, `run_428nktsej0yh`: $0.156 / 1944 out).
- For the orphaned run, that log line never appears — the bridge died before the next checkpoint.

So usage totals are checkpointed only periodically and finalized at reap. Any run that doesn't reach the next checkpoint **loses all its visible cost**, even though the underlying events are durably stored.

## Desired behavior

Operator-visible cost should be at least best-effort for any run with streamed events, regardless of terminal state.

Two complementary options:

1. **Compute on read** — when serving a run to the UI/API, if `runs.cost_usd` is null but events exist, derive it from `events` (sum the usage payloads). Cheap to add, surfaces existing data immediately. Could be a view or a fallback in the run repo's `findById`.
2. **Backfill on reconciliation** — when a run transitions to a terminal state (including the new ghost-run reconciler from warren-b1a9), recompute cost/tokens from `events` and write them to the row, so subsequent reads don't need the fallback.

Either alone fixes the symptom; ideally do (2) as the source of truth and (1) as a transitional fallback while old rows still have null totals.

## Acceptance

- A failed/cancelled run that has events shows a non-null cost and token totals in the UI matching the sum of streamed usage events.
- Backfilling `run_b9dz5jdsrvsv` (or replaying the scenario in a test) yields a positive cost roughly proportional to its 609 events / 454 usage payloads.

## Related

- warren-b1a9 — ghost-run reconciliation (this is the natural place to call the cost backfill).
- Today's incident — `run_b9dz5jdsrvsv` / burrow `run_ktz51n6n7ww3`.

title: Phase 2: thread plot_id through PlanRun (warren-fcc9) so plan-runs report to the originating Plot
description:
## Context

warren-000b Phase 2. Follow-up to **warren-000b** (Plot adoption epic) and **warren-fcc9** (PlanRun: serial plan execution). Phase 1 (`pl-2047`) wires Plot into single-run dispatch — `PLOT_ID` / `PLOT_ACTOR` env, `run_dispatched` emission, event mirroring at reap. This epic adds the same wiring to PlanRun so a plan-run launched from a Plot threads context through every child run and reports their lifecycle back to the Plot.

Independent of Plot at the PlanRun V1 boundary — composition is purely additive (per warren-fcc9 acceptance: 'Existing single-run dispatch path is unchanged; PlanRun is purely additive'). When both ship, this epic adds the Plot wiring.

## Scope

- Optional `plot_id` field on the PlanRun entity (DB column + API surface, mirroring the `runs.plot_id` column landed in pl-2047 / warren-a8c3).
- Dispatcher: when a PlanRun has `plot_id`, every child run dispatched by the coordinator inherits it — same env injection (`PLOT_ID` / `PLOT_ACTOR=agent:<name>:<run-id>`) the single-run path already does.
- One `run_dispatched` event per child landing in the Plot's event log (not just for the PlanRun parent — agents need to see each child run).
- A single `plan_run_dispatched` event on the Plot when the PlanRun itself starts, with payload {plan_run_id, plan_id, children_count}.
- Auto-transition: Plot status transitions to `done` when all PlanRun children close (per warren-000b §Scope, 'PlanRun composition' bullet). Defensive — only transitions if Plot is currently in `active` and write-ACL permits.
- Dispatch UI: PlanRun create form (warren-fcc9) accepts optional `plot_id` — autopopulated if the dispatch originates from a Plot detail page (Phase 3), free-form input until then.

## Gating

Surface only when BOTH `.plot/` is present in the project AND `.seeds/` is present (PlanRun's existing gate). Stack the gates; don't loosen either.

## Acceptance

- PlanRun row stores optional `plot_id`; POST /plan-runs accepts it; GET surfaces it.
- Dispatching a PlanRun with `plot_id` injects PLOT_ID + PLOT_ACTOR into every child run's sandbox env.
- A `plan_run_dispatched` event lands on the Plot at PlanRun start; one `run_dispatched` event per child as it spawns.
- Plot auto-transitions `active` → `done` when the final PlanRun child closes (and not before).
- PlanRun without `plot_id` is byte-identical to the warren-fcc9 baseline (no Plot leakage).
- POST /plan-runs with `plot_id` against a project without `.plot/` rejects with a typed error (same shape as the single-run validation in pl-2047 / warren-a8c3).

## Sequencing

Blocked on:
- **warren-fcc9** (PlanRun V1) closing — this epic composes on top of it.
- **pl-2047** closing — single-run Plot wiring is the substrate this reuses.

When both unblock, decompose this epic with `sd plan` the same way pl-2047 decomposed warren-000b Phase 1.

## References

- warren-000b — parent epic (Plot adoption, all three phases)
- warren-fcc9 — PlanRun (serial plan execution); §'Relationship to Plot' calls out this composition
- pl-2047 — Phase 1 decomposition (single-run dispatch wiring)
- ../plot/SPEC.md §6 — write-ACL (status_changed humans-only; auto-done transition uses warren's system actor)

title: Phase 3: shift warren UI from run-centric to Plot-centric (Plot list, detail, unified activity feed)
description:
## Context

warren-000b Phase 3 — the largest and most user-visible chunk. Follow-up to **warren-000b** (Plot adoption epic). Phase 1 (`pl-2047`) wired Plot into dispatch with no UI change. Phase 2 (`warren-06dc`) threads Plot through PlanRun. Phase 3 makes Plot the primary surface in the warren UI.

Driven by the post-V1 direction in [Agentic Networks](https://www.jayminwest.com/blog/12-agentic-networks): the unit users care about is no longer the run (orchestrator → worker) but the **Plot** (shared substrate where humans and agents coexist as peer nodes). Phase 3 rebuilds the UI around that unit.

## Scope (incremental — three sub-phases per warren-000b §Sequencing)

### 3a — Plot list page (additive)
- New `/plots` route in src/ui/.
- Server route: GET /plots returns Plot summaries (id, name, status, intent goal preview, attachments count, last-event timestamp, last-event actor).
- Card or table view, sortable by recency; filter by status (drafting / ready / active / done / archived).
- 'New Plot' button shells to `plot init` server-side and routes to the new Plot's detail page.
- No deprecation of /runs yet — both lists exist side by side.

### 3b — Plot detail page (largest)
- Route `/plots/:id` with three panels (per warren-000b §Scope):
  - **Intent panel** — goal / non-goals / constraints / success criteria, editable inline (humans only; agent actors get read-only with a 'open a question' affordance that creates a `question_posed` event).
  - **Substrate panel** — attachments list (seeds_issue, mulch_record, canopy_prompt, agent_run, gh_pr) with role labels; add/detach via UI.
  - **Activity feed** — unified timeline where human and agent events render with the same affordances; collapses repetitive event chains; shows actor in a stable visual slot.
- 'Run plan' button when an `sd plan` attachment is present — dispatches a PlanRun (warren-06dc) bound to this Plot.
- Status transition control (drafting → ready → active → done) gated on write-ACL — humans only.
- Question-answer affordance: open `question_posed` events surface as inline cards with an answer textarea; submit posts `question_answered` via the warren server (user actor).

### 3c — Deprecate run-list as primary surface (cleanup)
- /plots becomes the home page; /runs moves under a 'Runs' sidebar entry (kept for the standalone path that has no `.plot/`).
- Header navigation reorders accordingly.
- The PlanRun detail page (warren-fcc9) gets a back-link to its Plot when `plot_id` is set.

## Out of scope (file follow-ups)

- **Status-tag triggers** (warren-000b mentions this as 'post-Plot-V1, defer'): Plot transitioning to `ready` triggers a planner agent that calls `sd plan` and writes seeds back as attachments. Out of scope here; file follow-up when status-transition reliability is proven in 3a/3b.
- **Live Plot-event streaming** — Phase 1 chose JSONL-tail-at-reap (no live channel from sandbox to warren). UI 'Activity feed' will show events only after reap until that's revisited. Phase 3 should record what's missing; the live channel itself is a separate epic.
- **Multi-Plot cross-references** — when Plot A attaches Plot B, the UI should link them. Defer to a focused follow-up after the single-Plot surface is stable.

## Gating

Each sub-phase is independent and shippable. Gate the Plot navigation entry on the deployment having at least one project with `.plot/` (server-side discoverable via the per-project flag landed in pl-2047 / warren-4e20). Projects without any `.plot/` see the existing run-centric UI unchanged.

## Acceptance

- 3a: /plots route lists all Plots across projects with status + recency + new-Plot creation.
- 3b: /plots/:id renders intent, substrate, and unified activity in three panels; question-answer affordance round-trips through the Plot event log; 'Run plan' button dispatches a PlanRun bound to the Plot.
- 3c: /plots is the default landing for deployments with any `.plot/` projects; /runs remains reachable; PlanRun detail back-links to Plot when bound.
- ACL respected throughout: humans only can transition status, edit intent, answer questions, detach attachments; agent actors never construct those calls (defended by the facade landed in pl-2047 / warren-8b13).
- Existing single-run UI is byte-identical for projects without `.plot/` (no Plot-leak regressions).

## Sequencing

Blocked on:
- **warren-06dc** (Phase 2) closing — the 'Run plan' button on Plot detail dispatches via the Plot-aware PlanRun primitive.
- **pl-2047** (Phase 1) closing — the per-project `hasPlot` flag and the run.plot_id column landed there back the gating and the run→Plot links.

When both unblock, plan each sub-phase (3a / 3b / 3c) with its own `sd plan` decomposition — they're large enough that one mega-plan would be unwieldy.

## References

- warren-000b — parent epic
- warren-06dc — Phase 2 (PlanRun Plot wiring)
- pl-2047 — Phase 1 (single-run dispatch wiring)
- [Agentic Networks](https://www.jayminwest.com/blog/12-agentic-networks) — theoretical foundation
- ../plot/SPEC.md §3 (data model), §6 (write-ACL), §8 (views — informer/implementer/reviewer drive panel shaping)

title: burrow: parse body.env on POST /burrows so warren-e26f PLOT_ID/PLOT_ACTOR reach the sandbox
description: Warren's spawn flow (warren-e26f) sends {env: {PLOT_ID, PLOT_ACTOR}} on the burrow up POST body via BurrowClient.burrowsUp (src/burrow-client/client.ts). Burrow's server-side createBurrow handler (burrow-cli@0.3.1 src/server/handlers.ts:405) does NOT parse body.env into BurrowUpInput, so envOverrides stays empty in runUpCommand and the values never reach SandboxProfile.setEnv. Result: the in-sandbox plot CLI (and any future per-run env contract from warren) sees PLOT_ID empty. Acceptance scenario 25 (warren-4e06) detects this gap — the env-reaches-sandbox + workspace plot append + plot.* mirror assertions are currently soft-skipped pending this fix. Once burrow accepts body.env (parse → BurrowUpInput.envOverrides → resolveEnv overrides → setEnv), bump @os-eco/burrow-cli in package.json + bun.lock + Dockerfile (double-pin per the burrow-cli convention) and flip the conditional assertions in scenario 25 back to hard required.

title: Update warren Plot ID test fixtures + SPEC §11.O path examples after plot rename (plot-ab1f)
description:
## Context

Blocked on plot's rename of Plot ID prefix `pl-` → `plot-` (filed as `plot-ab1f` in ../plot). Once plot ships the new format, warren's test fixtures, SPEC examples, and one acceptance scenario's doc comments should be updated for consistency. **No production code changes required** — warren does not validate Plot ID format and `runs.plot_id` is opaque text.

## Why this matters

Concrete evidence of the collision in this very repo: `src/db/repos/runs.test.ts:113` uses `plotId: "pl-2047"` as a Plot ID fixture, while `src/burrow-client/client.ts:135` references `pl-2047 step 4` as the **seeds plan ID** for warren-000b. Same string, two different ID types, in one codebase. The rename eliminates this entire class of accident.

## Changes

### Test fixtures (rename to `plot-<8 lowercase alnum>` form)

- `src/plot-client/client.test.ts` — replace `pl-deadbeef` (×4)
- `src/plot-client/handle.test.ts` — replace `pl-test0001` (×2)
- `src/server/handlers.test.ts:871,893` — replace `plot_id = 'pl-2047'` and the matching assertion
- `src/db/repos/runs.test.ts:113` — replace `plotId: \"pl-2047\"`

Suggested replacements: `plot-deadbeef`, `plot-test0001`, `plot-2047abc` (or any 8-char lowercase alnum). The literal value is irrelevant to the tests — they only need to be syntactically valid Plot IDs under the new regex.

### SPEC.md §11.O path examples

Update the flow-diagram comments around lines 1738–1755 from `.plot/pl-<id>.events.jsonl` / `.plot/pl-*.events.jsonl` / `.plot/pl-*.json` to use `plot-<id>` / `plot-*` filename patterns. These are doc examples of plot's on-disk layout, so they should track plot's new convention.

### Scenario 25 doc comments (no logic changes)

`scripts/acceptance/scenarios/25-plot-roundtrip.ts` is already format-agnostic at runtime — it reads the Plot ID from `plot list --json` (line 336) and composes file paths via template literal (line 165: \`join(..., \`\${plotId}.events.jsonl\`)\`). Only the documentation comments at lines 19, 33, 38, 219, 320 reference the literal `pl-<id>` filename pattern and should be flipped to `plot-<id>`.

Do **not** touch line 2's `(warren-4e06, pl-2047)` — that `pl-2047` is the seeds plan ID for the plot integration work.

## Do NOT touch

- Seeds plan ID references throughout warren (`pl-fef5`, `pl-bb70`, `pl-9ba1`, `pl-2f15`, `pl-5d74`, `pl-cb3e`, `pl-2c59`, `pl-f4ea`, `pl-2047`-as-plan-id, etc.). Those live in seeds' namespace and stay as-is.
- The §11.O section header `### 11.O Plot integration (pl-2047, 2026-05-17)` — `pl-2047` there is the **seeds plan ID** for the plot integration work, not a Plot ID.
- DB schema — `runs.plot_id` is opaque text. No migration.
- `src/plot-client/` production code — no regex validation; nothing to change.

## Acceptance

1. All five test fixture sites updated to `plot-<8>` form.
2. SPEC §11.O path examples use `plot-*` filename patterns.
3. Scenario 25's five doc comments use `plot-<id>` filename pattern.
4. `bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` clean.
5. Scenario 25 still passes against a plot release that emits the new ID format.
6. `git grep \"pl-test\\|pl-dead\\|plot_id = 'pl-\\|plotId: \\\"pl-\" src/` returns no results in production code (test fixtures only).

## Blocked on

`plot-ab1f` in ../plot must ship first so the new `@os-eco/plot` release defines the new format. Warren can update fixtures pre-bump (they're just strings warren doesn't validate), but doing it after plot ships keeps the rename atomic ecosystem-wide.

This step is the smallest foundation and blocks the API surface (step 6) which validates `project.hasSeeds`.

title: DB schema + state machine: add `plan_runs` table to BOTH dialect schemas with columns (id TEXT PK, plan_id TEXT NOT NULL, project_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, agent_name TEXT NOT NULL, prompt_template TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'work on sd {seed_id}', ref TEXT, provider_override TEXT, model_override TEXT, dispatcher_handle TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'operator', trigger TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'manual', state TEXT NOT NULL CHECK enforced via TS-only narrowing per mx-2ab984, failure_reason TEXT, created_at TEXT NOT NULL, started_at TEXT, ended_at TEXT) with indexes (plan_runs_project_state ON (project_id, state), plan_runs_state ON state); add `plan_run_children` table (plan_run_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES plan_runs(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, seq INTEGER NOT NULL, seed_id TEXT NOT NULL, run_id TEXT REFERENCES runs(id) ON DELETE SET NULL, state TEXT NOT NULL ('pending'|'dispatched'|'running'|'pr_open'|'merged'|'failed'|'skipped'), created_at TEXT NOT NULL, updated_at TEXT NOT NULL, started_at TEXT, ended_at TEXT, pr_merged_at TEXT, failure_reason TEXT) with composite PK (plan_run_id, seq) and indexes (plan_run_children_run ON run_id, plan_run_children_state ON (plan_run_id, state)); add the new state-enum tuples PLAN_RUN_STATES = ['queued','running','succeeded','failed','cancelled'] and PLAN_RUN_CHILD_STATES = ['pending','dispatched','running','pr_open','merged','failed','skipped'] to src/db/schema/columns.ts; add the table + index names to TABLE_NAMES / INDEX_NAMES (mx-de2250 — drift test imports from here); regenerate migrations via `bun run --silent drizzle-kit generate --config drizzle.config.sqlite.ts` and the postgres counterpart, then hand-rename to descriptive `0015_add_plan_runs.sql` / `0008_add_plan_runs.sql` per the rename convention (mx-b4f9b5); migrations.test.ts must roundtrip; the drift test (src/db/schema/drift.test.ts) must pass without further hand-editing; build new repo `src/db/repos/plan-runs.ts` (PlanRunsRepo) on DrizzleAdapter following the dialect-polymorphic pattern (mx-1d9f7a) with methods: create({planRunRow, children}) (single tx via the adapter's tx helper that lands the parent row + N child rows atomically; mx-d5cf19 sqlite-async-tx pattern is the substrate), getById(id), listByProjectAndState, listActive() returning ('queued','running'), transitionTo(id, state, opts: {failureReason?, startedAt?, endedAt?}) with the state-machine guarded the same way RunsRepo.transitionTo guards runs (mx-a5432a), updateChild({planRunId, seq, patch}), and pickNextPending(planRunId) returning the lowest-seq pending child or null; mirror the partial-input pattern from RunsRepo.attachBurrow (mx-0a7a65) for child patches; extend src/db/repos/index.ts createRepos to construct + expose `planRuns`; ship src/db/repos/plan-runs.test.ts with: state-machine guards (no invalid transition), insertChildren idempotent on identical seq, pickNextPending returns null when all children !=pending, listActive omits terminal rows, and the tx rolls back the children when the parent insert violates a constraint (drop a duplicate id mid-tx). This is the substrate for steps 3, 5, 6.
